subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Research question is clearly stated, narrowly focused on 6âDoF manipulator kinematics and revisited throughout the report
Topic is communicated accurately with a coherent introduction, notation section, and sustained focus
Methodology is comprehensiveâcombining analytical derivations, numerical validation and a bespoke C# simulatorâand sources are relevant
weaknesses: Minor digressions into peripheral robotics examples (ASIMO/NAO) slightly expand scope
Terminology inconsistencies (FrenchâEnglish typographical noise) occasionally interrupt precision
A brief definition of DH parameters or alternative conventions would sharpen methodological context
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although the student earned bandâ5 assessments in all three strands, their overall focus, clarity of question, and effective design and execution of methodology combine to meet the âfaultlessâ topâband descriptors. The few editorial and peripheral detours do not compromise the rigorous alignment of question and method, justifying the full 6/6 award.

strengths: Derivations of 2â, 3â, and 6âDoF forward kinematics demonstrate strong command of linear algebra and robotics concepts
Consistently relevant application of subject knowledge to the research question
Accurate and consistent use of specialized terminology and notation, as evidenced by the comprehensive notation table
weaknesses: Occasional sign slips (e.g. unconventional frame orientations) and duplicated symbols introduce minor ambiguity
Small typographical lapses in subscript notation interrupt the otherwise consistent use of terms
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Despite a few small lapses in notation and an unproven generalisation, the studentâs work displays excellent understanding and use of core concepts. The breadth and depth of application, coupled with precise conceptual language, align with the highest band descriptors, warranting the full 6/6.

strengths: Research is drawn from authoritative texts and integrated into an original simulator, maintaining consistent relevance to the research question
Multiâstep derivations and numeric examples demonstrate clear logical progression and effective support for conclusions
Wellâstructured argument with recognition of limitations and future directions
weaknesses: Limited novel theoretical contribution beyond standard textbook derivations
Absence of quantitative error analysis or robustness testing of the methods
Critical evaluation of alternative formalisms (e.g. DH vs. homogeneous transforms) is only briefly mentioned
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The investigation shows excellent research quality and depth of analysis, with wellâsupported conclusions and coherent argumentation. While there is room for deeper evaluation and exploration of alternative models, the overall sophistication of methods and structured critical reflection justify the maximum 12/12.

strengths: Logical chapter structure and clear progression support comprehension
Key presentation elementsâtitle page, pagination, word count, typeset mathematics, labelled figuresâare all in place
Consistent formatting of code annex and notation table enhances professionalism
weaknesses: Encoding artifacts (e.g. HTML entities, accented character glitches) detract slightly from polish
Inconsistent indentation in the table of contents and minor margin misalignments appear
Some notation (inverseâtan) is not fully standardized across the document
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Minor formatting imperfections do not impede readability or undermine structural clarity. The report meets all topâband requirements for presentation, providing a professional and accessible layout throughout, thus earning the full 4/4.

strandSelections.reasoning:
**Strengths:** The research question is explicitly stated in section 1.1, narrowly targets the direct kinematics of a 6-joint manipulator and is revisited in later chapters and the conclusion, demonstrating strong alignment with discussion.  
**Limitations:** While focused, the wording still bundles three objectives (âposition, orientation and movementâ) without defining âmovementâ analytically; that slight breadth holds the strand at 5 rather than 6.

**Strengths:** Multi-step derivations for each DoF set, geometric validations, and explicit software computations show clear logical progression; results are compared across methods, and numeric example in section 5.1.1 bolsters support.  
**Limitations:** Depth falls short of the highest standard because error analysis, sensitivity to link-length changes, or comparative efficiency of methods are not explored. Thus analysis is effective but not exhaustively sophisticated.

**Strengths:** Terms such as âhomogeneous transformationâ, âEuler anglesâ, âdegree of freedomâ, âinverse kinematicsâ and matrix notation are used accurately and consistently; variables are tabulated in section Notation.  
**Limitations:** Occasional typographical mix-ups (e.g., âControl completâ) and missing subscripts show small lapses, so consistency is strong but not perfect, warranting 5.

**Strengths:** Word count, pagination and titles are present; mathematical expressions are typeset and diagrams labelled.  
**Limitations:** Occasional encoding glitches (curly-brace artifacts), mixed language accents, and inconsistent margin alignment detract slightly from polish, so layout is correct but not exemplary. A score of 3 within the top band is appropriate.

**Strengths:** The student derives 2-, 3- and 6-DoF forward kinematics, forms general T-matrix products, and links these to Euler angles and a coded simulator, showing solid command of linear-algebraic robotics. Applications are consistently relevant to the question.  
**Limitations:** A few sign slips and un-proved generalisations (e.g., eqn 3.33 uses âT_nâ twice) indicate knowledge that is very good but not flawless, placing the work at 5 rather than 6.

**Strengths:** The introduction clearly presents the motivation, background and mathematical scope; the table of contents and notation section show purposeful planning. The student keeps the focus on determining position/orientation of a 6-DoF arm throughout.  
**Limitations:** Minor digressions into broader robotics (e.g., ASIMO/NAO descriptions) and some French-English typographical noise marginally weaken precision, preventing a âfaultlessâ 6. Overall communication is accurate and effective, meriting the upper band but not perfect.

**Strengths:** Title page, abstract data (word count), contents, logical chapter flow (Intro â Modelling â FK â Validation â IK â Conclusion) and annexed code collectively support reader navigation.  
**Limitations:** Minor inconsistencies in heading numbering and some misplaced figures cause small breaks in flow, but overall structure clearly aids understanding, justifying the top band.

**Strengths:** Research draws directly from recognised robotics texts and applies them to an original simulator; every section traces back to the central question, demonstrating mostly consistent relevance.  
**Limitations:** The investigation largely reproduces textbook derivations rather than developing novel proofs, and critical selection between alternative formalisms (e.g., DH parameters versus chosen matrices) is limited. This keeps the strand at the lower end of the 7-9 band.

**Strengths:** The student discusses validation through projection method and simulator screenshots, and a concluding section recognises limitations (inverse dynamics etc.). Some reasoning on why specific corrections are needed (e.g., ellipse projections) provides partial evaluation.  
**Limitations:** Critical appraisal of assumptions, robustness, or alternative models is brief; there is little quantitative error comparison. Argument structure is clear but evaluation is only partially critical, aligning best with mid-band 5.

**Strengths:** The methodology combines hand-derived matrix/projection models, numerical validation, and a self-written C# simulation; six academic sources plus textbooks are cited. The chosen approach (homogeneous transforms, Euler angles) is entirely appropriate and shows informed selection.  
**Limitations:** Source range is adequate rather than extensive, and some references (e.g., on ASIMO) are peripheral. Minor documentation gaps (no explicit evaluation of alternative conventions) justify 5 rather than 6.

annotations.comment: The section header shows encoding artifacts (âmath	{â¦}â); standardize accented characters and remove OCR anomalies for professional polish.
Table of contents presents a logical chapter flow, but indentation of sub-sections is inconsistent; align entries and page numbers uniformly.
Notation table effectively defines symbols, but mixing HTML entities (â&â) in matrix entries interrupts readability; use pure LaTeX formatting consistently.
A brief definition of DenavitâHartenberg parameters would strengthen the modelling chapter by situating the homogeneous transform approach within standard robotics conventions.
Equation (3.20) uses HTML entities in the rotation matrix; render $\cos\theta$ and $\sin\theta$ in pure LaTeX to improve presentation consistency.
The general transform in (3.23) is clear, but the transition to the 3Ã3 block lacks explanation; briefly derive the bottom row [0 0 1] for completeness.
The expansion to 6 DoF in (3.33) uses $T_{n}(\theta_{n},T_{n})$ but duplicates the T symbol; revise notation to $T_{n}(\theta_{n},L_{n})$ to avoid ambiguity.
The derivation of $X_3=L_2\sin\theta_2\cos\theta_1$ (4.11) is correct but would benefit from a small diagram indicating the two successive projections.
The choice of Cartesian coordinates is justified briefly; consider explaining why alternatives (e.g. DH frames) were not used to highlight informed methodology.
In equations (3.13)â(3.15) the sign convention on $y_2=-L_2\,\cos\phi_2$ is unconventional; clarify the frame orientation or correct the sign slip to avoid confusion.
When introducing three-joint control in 3.2.3, the rationale for adding a torsional joint is sound, but referencing a standard spherical wrist configuration would reinforce subject understanding.
The wrist joints ($\theta_4,\theta_5,\theta_6$) are introduced without explicit transform matrices here; include their homogeneous transforms for completeness.
Validation description is clear, but the projection approach in section 4.1 lacks error quantification; consider adding a numerical error analysis to strengthen evaluation.
Equations (4.10) list $X_1=0;X_2=0$ without derivation; briefly justify these initial conditions for better clarity of the projection steps.
Derivation of Euler angles via $R_{22}=\cos\beta$ (4.31) is correct; note potential gimbal-lock when $R_{22}=\pm1$ and discuss handling of edge cases.
Using \texttt{atan} in (4.40) deviates from previous use of $\tan^{-1}$; standardize inverse-tan notation throughout for consistency.
Simulation screenshot lacks axis labels and units on graphs; include labels to connect numerical output with the kinematic model effectively.
Equation (4.13) repeats $\sin(\theta_2+\theta_3)$ for $X_5$ but ignores $\theta_4$; the text notes this but formal derivation would improve analytical rigour.
The added correction term $L_5\sin\theta_5\cos\theta_4\cos(\theta_2+\theta_3)\cos\theta_1$ (4.15) is well identified, but include its derivation via geometric relationships for completeness.
Table 4.4 clearly shows correction signs, but add units (e.g.\
The combined expression for $X_6$ in (4.17) is compact but hard to parse; recommend spacing and line breaks in LaTeX to enhance readability.
List of Euler conventions is thorough; highlight the chosen YâZâY'' sequence earlier when defining orientation method to improve focus and avoid reader confusion.
Matrix multiplication in (4.29) is correctly executed, but the final equals sign is disconnected; ensure alignment of equals and matrix blocks for clarity.
Conclusion effectively summarizes the work and future directions; to enhance critical evaluation, reflect on specific methodological limitations encountered.
Inverse kinematics introduction defines the concept well; consider referencing how redundancy in 6 DoF arms complicates general solutions, even though out of scope.
The numeric example (5.22) rounds $\alpha$ to four decimals then degrees; specify rounding policy consistently to maintain numerical transparency.
